questions.scenario: A developer asks for Owner on the production subscription because they need to restart one app. Which response best matches the review habit?

options.text: Ask what action is needed, which resource scope is needed, and whether a narrower role can satisfy the task.
Approve Owner because production incidents should avoid access questions.
Deny all access because users should never touch production.
Move the app to a new tenant so the request no longer mentions production.

questions.explanation: Access review should match the task to the smallest useful role and scope. It should not jump straight to broad production ownership.

questions.scenario: A background worker needs to read from Key Vault at runtime. The request asks to grant access to a human developer account so the worker can keep running. What is the safer direction?

options.text: Use a non-human workload identity for the worker and grant that identity the needed access.
Use the developer account because it already passed sign-in checks.
Use a shared password in app settings so no Entra ID object is needed.
Grant access to every engineer in the group because one of them may debug it later.

questions.explanation: Runtime software should have its own identity. Human accounts are for people actions, not long-lived app access.

questions.scenario: A good access request is ready for review. Which facts should it include?

options.text: Principal type and object ID.
Requested role.
Requested scope.
A promise that the requester will remove access later, without an owner or review date.

questions.explanation: A reviewer needs the principal, role, and scope. Temporary access should also have an owner and review habit, not just a vague promise.

questions.scenario: A user can sign in to Azure Portal but cannot list resources in `rg-devpolaris-orders-prod`. What does this show?

options.text: Authentication succeeded, but authorization for that resource scope is still missing or too narrow.
The user's password is wrong because listing resources is part of sign-in.
The resource group does not exist because signed-in users can always list every group.
The tenant region is incompatible with the user's location.

questions.explanation: Sign-in proves identity authentication. Azure RBAC still decides whether that identity can act at a scope.

questions.scenario: A service principal was created for a temporary migration. The migration finished two weeks ago, but the service principal still has production write access. What should the review habit catch?

options.text: The non-human identity still has access after its job ended and should be reviewed or removed.
The service principal should be converted into a user so it appears in people reviews.
The production resources should be moved to a new subscription so the old access stops being visible.
The access is safe because non-human identities cannot make production changes.

questions.explanation: Service principals are powerful principals. Temporary workload access needs the same review discipline as people access.
